title: host.containers.internal accessing host port served by Windows app
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

This is about latest Podman for Windows and `host.containers.internal`. The capability of `host.containers.internal` seems to differ from docker's. My tests have excluded the firewall factor.
- `host.containers.internal` can access ports exposed to host in other containers. Cool.
- However, `host.containers.internal` cannot access ports served by Windows applications.

I'm guessing it may because `host.containers.internal` refers to the Fedora host than Windows host.

On the other hand, WSL 2 has a special domain name `$(hostname).local` to access the Windows part. ($hostname refers to Windows host name rather than Linux's.)
- Within a WSL session, `$(hostname).local` can access host Windows ports, no matter exposed by Windows or WSL. Cool.
- Within a podman pod, `$(hostname).local` can access host Windows ports served by Windows applications. Cool.
- However, within a podman pod, `$(hostname).local` cannot access host ports served by podman.

This surprise me. I cannot explain why. But at least I got a way to access Windows app ports from podman. The tricky thing is that, unlike `host.docker.internal` which I can use for all scenarios, I have to split config depending on what it is accessing to.

I wish both of them work out of box. Or, at least one of them works.
Thanks for the good work.

comments.body: Any chance you can find out what IP address Docker's `host.containers.internal` points to, versus ours? Knowing how their configuration differs would help significantly.

In podman:
172.24.126.173  host.containers.internal
nameserver 172.24.112.1 // It is what WSL's $(hostname).local resolves to.

In Docker:
host.docker.internal resolves to 192.168.65.2
while nameserver  192.168.65.5

I think docker is playing some magic here. It uses its own nameserver than WSL's, and the `host.docker.internal` access is forwarded to Windows side without roadblock of Windows Firewall. (As it uses its own nameserver, WSL's `$(hostname).local` does not work in docker. But it is OK as `host.docker.internal` works for all scenarios.)

On the other hand, podman's seem to be within the WSL realm, following what's provided by WSL. In this case, I think the WSL's `$(hostname).local` should work, but it does not work with ports served by podman, strangely.

I think there are 2 possible paths which podman for windows can choose from:
1. the Docker's approach which creates a secret tunnel back to Windows. This makes it feel rather native on the Windows side. But this may be non-trivial to implement.
2. the WSL path so that `host.containers.internal` is equivalent to WSL's `$(hostname).local`. (Of course need to address the issue of access podman exposed ports.) The benefit is that it will be consistent with WSL and inherit any new feature of WSL, while the drawback is that WSL has not yet solved the Windows Firewall issue, which treats WSL ethernet as public network, thus blocks non-standard port access from WSL by default.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Some update about latest status:
* It seems "$(hostname).local" is no longer supported by WSL
* nameserver IP (the 172.* IP of the Windows host for WSL) still works
* There are people saying the access is no longer blocked as part of supporting WSLg - I tried but no success. Access from WSL2 to high ports of Windows side is still blocked by Windows Firewall by default. But it can be unblocked by executing `Set-NetFirewallProfile -Name Public -DisabledInterfaceAliases "vEthernet (WSL)"`

> Some update about latest status:
> 
>     * It seems "$(hostname).local" is no longer supported by WSL
> 
>     * nameserver IP (the 172.* IP of the Windows host for WSL) still works
> 
>     * There are people saying the access is no longer blocked as part of supporting WSLg - I tried but no success. Access from WSL2 to high ports of Windows side is still blocked by Windows Firewall by default. But it can be unblocked by executing `Set-NetFirewallProfile -Name Public -DisabledInterfaceAliases "vEthernet (WSL)"`

Thanks for the info.

I have a similar problem: From within the podman VM I need to access a Windows port (This is a cntlm proxy listening on :3128 on Windows). 
Is there any way you found to make a connection from within podman VM to Windows without having to touch firewall settings?

Pops this up a bit. There is still no change on this.

Any progress of WSL does not help either, because `host.containers.internal` binds to the WSL IP, which cannot access anything on the Windows side. It is basically the same as installing Linux version of Podman inside WSL, although we expect Podman Windows to be replacement of Docker on Windows.

I think `host.containers.internal` should bind to gateway IP of WSL, which is show in the first line of `ip route show`. See https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/networking#mirrored-mode-networking

It may still be blocked by Windows Firewall by default when accessing ports served from Windows side, but there are already easy ways to allow it.

Terrible workaround follows.

Get a shell on the podman VM:

~~~
podman machine ssh
~~~

From this shell:

~~~sh
sudo yum install socat
ip route
socat TCP-LISTEN:8000,fork TCP:$ip:8000
~~~

where `$ip` is the address found in `ip route` (`default via …`)
Is there any progress on this?

> Terrible workaround follows.
> 
> Get a shell on the podman VM:
> 
> ```
> podman machine ssh
> ```
> 
> From this shell:
> 
> ```shell
> sudo yum install socat
> ip route
> socat TCP-LISTEN:8000,fork TCP:$ip:8000
> ```
> 
> where `$ip` is the address found in `ip route` (`default via …`)

@randomstuff  I suppose you'll have to do this for every port you want to expose? :)

I  modified the .wslconfig , and added such configuration:
```
[wsl2]
networkingMode=mirrored
```
Then restarted wsl and podman machine, I found that ` telnet host.containers.internal 3001` was refused, but when I tried `telnet 127.0.0.1 3001` , it worked.

the working workaround for me is the one described here: https://stackoverflow.com/a/79099459

I am forced to calculate windows host IP in the following way (GitBash script), but bear in mind to not use mirrored networking mode as then that "WSL" Interface will not be available (the default WSL networking mode will make that interface available)

run-containers.sh

```bash
HOST_IP=$(Get-NetIpAddress | where { $_.InterfaceAlias -Like '*WSL*' -and $_.AddressFamily -EQ "IPv4" } | select -ExpandProperty IPAddress)
podman compose up
```

compose.yaml

```yaml
    # ...
    extra_hosts:
      - "host.docker.internal:${HOST_IP}"
```

then when running `curl http://host.docker.internal:8080 -v` from the container I am able to access the service running on Windows machine as expected.

in the past, I also needed exactly the same / that workaround for Docker, but Docker fixed it after some time. Now it's time for Podman to also make this IP calculation fixed as well :) I hope somebody could take care on this.

@krystian-panek-vmltech that link's Solution number 2 looks like a good option for a permanent solution if Podman implements the bootstrapping for this. Thank you for posting this.

But there is still a problem with that implementation. That does allow you to access applications running on the Windows host, but does not allow your containers to connect to other containers that are port mapped to Windows, even though it seems like it should. The port can be accessed if you curl from windows, but not if you curl host.docker.internal:exposed-port in the container.
I have same issue, it makes podman not usable for me :/

I have an update to share... I have found that this fixes the issue for me:

- Enable `User mode networking (traffic relayed by a user process)`.
- Edit the host IP address in your Podman Machine.
  - In Powershell, open a prompt to the podman machine: `podman machine ssh`
  - Edit the containers config file: `vi /usr/share/containers/containers.conf`
  - Find the entry for `#host_containers_internal_ip = ""` and edit:
    - Remove the comment symbol (#) at the beginning of the line.
    - Modify the line like so: `host_containers_internal_ip = "192.168.127.254"`
    - Save file and exit the shell.
  - Back in the Powershell on the host, issue commands:
    - podman machine stop
    - wsl --shutdown
    - podman machine start

The main trick here is setting `host_containers_internal_ip = "192.168.127.254"`. Feels like this should be a default setting to resolve this issue.

For anyone that is using WSL mirrored networking (as described [here](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/networking#mirrored-mode-networking)), I found using `--network=pasta:--map-gw` and the gateway IP as shown with `ip route show` on your Podman machine will allow connection to host services. 

E.g. if you have port 8080 open on your host and your gateway IP is `192.168.0.1`, the following should work:
```
$ podman run --rm -t --network=pasta:--map-gw busybox nc -z -v 192.168.0.1 8080
192.168.0.1 (192.168.0.1:8080) open
```

You can combine this with the above comment and set your `host_containers_internal_ip` to allow `host.containers.internal` usage.

@shastaxc
I agree with your solution. This should be default in podman
Any progress on it? We have faced the same issue for a long time 😓
Any update in this issue? My team is facing the same problem here...
It looks like it isn't solved, I tried different workarounds but at the end I just give up with podman, this shouldn't be this hard, uninstalling podman

I had the following problem:

- Windows 11, WSL2, podman desktop running a devcontainer
- devcontainer needed to access 127.0.0.1:9000 in Windows

Solution:

- In a non-admin DOS prompt:  `podman machine init --user-mode-networking`
- In compose.yml
` extra_hosts:`
` - "host.docker.internal:IP_ADDRESS"` where IP_ADDRESS is the public windows IP address.
- Add forwarding with netsh (admin powershell)
`netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 add listenaddress=0.0.0.0 listenport=9000 connectaddress=127.0.0.1 connectport=9000`

What this does:
- The container can use the expression `http://host.docker.internal:9000` to hit 127.0.0.1:9000 on windows.  This is my HTTP_PROXY environment variable.
- If the user changes networks (i.e. takes a laptop from work to home), they can edit the definition of 'host.docker.internal' in '/etc/hosts' to match the IP at home and everything will work.
- If the proxy changes ports, they can enter another 'netsh' command with the correct 'connectport' value.

What would be nice:
- A way for podman to route traffic to 127.0.0.1 on Windows 11 + WSL2.  This works on MacOSX.  I couldn't get WSL2 'mirrored' mode to work.
- If I have to use user-mode-networking, a way to lookup the public IP of the host within the devcontainer.

thanks to [shastaxc](https://github.com/shastaxc) solution
[https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/14933#issuecomment-2701706051](url)
I make it clearly

## open user-mode-networking,
this step make container can access host port by 192.168.127.254,
this is enough for most cases.
if you need new vm
```cmd
podman machine init --user-mode-networking
```
if you use old vm
```cmd
podman machine stop
podman machine set --user-mode-networking podman-machine-default
wsl --shutdown
podman machine start
```

## set host_containers_internal_ip to 192.168.127.254
this step set host.containers.internal to 192.168.127.254
```cmd
podman machine ssh
sudo vi /usr/share/containers/containers.conf
```
add a line host_containers_internal_ip = "192.168.127.254" to file /usr/share/containers/containers.conf
save and quit

## run container with pasta:--map-gw
this step set 127.0.0.1 to 192.168.127.254
```cmd
podman run --network pasta:--map-gw -t localhost/some-image:latest
```
